context.description: A reflective philosophical passage argues that dreams are symbolic fragments of waking consciousness and that their meaning lies less in interpretation than in their formation. It extends the analogy to waking life, asking whether fragments of the superego may also shape a coherent lived world. The section heading introduces a cautionary turn about the limits and contradictions of such analogies.
normalization_notes: Translated German visible text into English control language.
Section heading retained from source: Es ist nicht alles Gold was glänzt.

fields.section_heading: Es ist not all gold that glitters

rows.heading_text: Es is not all gold that glitters
rows.text_content: Reflective section on the limits of dream-to-waking analogies and the symbolic formation of consciousness.

rows.paragraph_text: Dreams are presented as fragments of waking consciousness that become symbolic in dream form.
The passage shifts from interpreting dream symbols to examining how they originate in consciousness.
It extends the analogy to waking life by asking whether fragments of the superego may shape a coherent world of experience.
The opening of the section warns that such analogies create contradictions and should be handled cautiously.

content.free_text: book_page nonfiction_philosophy dreams_and_altered_states Book page from Bewusstseinsreisen - Version 2 on dream symbolism, waking consciousness, and the superego. Section heading: Es ist nicht alles Gold was glänzt. Philosophical argument about the origin of symbols and the limits of analogy.
